name: Finland
summary: The world's happiest country with top-tier rule of law (WJP 3rd), a strong AI ecosystem (Aalto, Silo AI, Nokia, Supercell), a 2-year graduate job-search permit, residence-based universal healthcare, a top passport and dual citizenship retained both ways (allowed; Finland on Bangladesh's DNC list) — but tempered for this profile by a hard Finnish/Swedish language burden for status, a Finns-Party-driven restrictive turn (citizenship and PR tightened in 2025–26, PR now 6 years), high tuition (~€17k/yr at Aalto), high tax, a moderate Muslim community with no grand mosque, and Bangladeshi visa biometrics routed through New Delhi. Derived overall sits just below the 60% inclusion line.

links.title: Migri (Finnish Immigration Service) — official
Study in Finland — official national portal

job-market.summary: A genuine AI/ML hub (Aalto, Silo AI, Nokia, Supercell) with strong salaries (AI €56–121k) and a 12,000-worker ICT gap where 45% of roles are hard to fill, though entry-level roles stay competitive and the market is Helsinki-centric.

pros.text: A genuine AI/ML hub (Aalto, Silo AI, Nokia network-AI, Supercell ML); the Helsinki ICT sector needs ~12,000 new workers by 2026 with 45% of roles hard to fill.
Solid pay: AI/ML engineers ~€56k (junior) to €121k+ (principal); software engineers ~€55k–75k in Helsinki.

cons.text: Entry-level roles are very competitive (200+ applicants each) and employers prefer experienced candidates with a local network.
The market is Helsinki-centric and has seen tech-sector job cuts despite record revenue.

links.title: Jobicy — AI Engineer salary, Finland (2025)
Nucamp — Most in-demand tech jobs in Finland (2025)

visa-access.summary: Modest funds requirements and online application, but Bangladeshi applicants must give biometrics at VFS New Delhi (no Finland centre in Dhaka), and a 2026 rule lets study permits be revoked if a student claims social assistance.

pros.text: Applications are filed online via Enter Finland and the proof-of-funds bar is low (~€560/month); processing is typically 1–3 months.
No Bangladesh-specific visa ban.

cons.text: Finland has no application centre in Bangladesh — biometrics and identity verification are done at VFS Global / the Finnish Embassy in New Delhi, requiring travel to India.
From 2026, a study-based residence permit can be revoked if the student is found to have received basic social assistance.

links.title: Finland abroad — Residence permits to Finland (Bangladesh, official)
Study in Finland — student residence permit (official)

citizenship.summary: Dual citizenship is retained both ways and the passport is elite (Henley 4th), with a 5-year path if you have B1 Finnish/Swedish — but the language is hard and the Finns-Party government tightened the rules in 2025.

pros.text: Dual citizenship works both ways: Finland imposes no renunciation and Finland is on Bangladesh's DNC list, so a naturalising Bangladeshi keeps BD nationality.
Naturalisation is possible after 5 years' residence if you hold B1 Finnish/Swedish (YKI level 3); the Finnish passport ranks joint 4th on Henley 2026 (~185).

cons.text: The B1 requirement is in Finnish or Swedish — both difficult languages — and without it the residence requirement is 8 years (tightened from 1 Oct 2024).
Finns-Party-driven reforms (in force 17 Dec 2025) added an income requirement, stricter conduct rules and expanded grounds for citizenship deprivation.

links.title: Migri — Finnish citizenship FAQ (official)
Migri — Language skills requirement (official)

post-study-work.summary: Graduates get a 2-year residence permit to look for work or start a business, continuous (A permit) if the prior permit was, and available to each graduating spouse.

pros.text: After graduation, the permit can be extended to a 2-year residence permit to look for work or start a business (an A/continuous permit if the prior one was).
Both graduating spouses can each hold their own, with full work rights during the search.

cons.text: Conversion to long-term skilled work still needs a qualifying job, and the broader migration climate has tightened.

links.title: Migri — Residence permit to look for work (official)
Study in Finland — opportunities after graduation (official)

spouse-family.summary: Family members get residence with work rights and free schooling, and if both spouses study each holds an independent track.

pros.text: Family members of a worker/student can obtain residence with the right to work, and children attend high-quality public school free of charge.
If both spouses study, each holds their own permit and 2-year PSW route.

cons.text: Family migration requires proving sufficient means, and the dependent spouse's work access during the study phase is conditional.

links.title: Migri — Family member of a person who has a residence permit (official)

msc-study.summary: Top-tier English-taught CS/AI at Aalto and Helsinki with generous student work (30 hrs/week) and a master qualifying for PR without the year requirement, but non-EU tuition is high (~€17k/yr).

pros.text: Aalto's Machine Learning, Data Science and AI MSc (and CS MSc) are top-tier in the Nordics; students may work ~30 hrs/week on average.
A Finnish master's qualifies for permanent residence without the standard years-of-residence requirement (with language and 2 years' work).

cons.text: Non-EU tuition is high (~€17,000/yr at Aalto) with only a small number of Excellence Scholarship waivers.

links.title: Aalto University — ML, Data Science and AI MSc (tuition, official)
Aalto University — Tuition fees and scholarships (official)

pr-pathway.summary: Permanent residence was extended to 6 years from January 2026 (Finnish-degree graduates may skip the year requirement) and requires Finnish/Swedish plus work — a tightened, language-heavy route.

pros.text: Graduates of a Finnish master's may obtain permanent residence without meeting the standard years-of-residence requirement, given Finnish/Swedish proficiency and 2 years' work.

cons.text: From January 2026 the continuous-residence requirement for PR rose from 4 to 6 years, plus a Finnish/Swedish-language condition.
The Finns-Party-led restrictive direction reduces predictability.

links.title: Migri — Amendments to Aliens Act on permanent residence permits 2026 (official)

income-cost.summary: Good tech salaries are offset by a high tax wedge and high tuition; Helsinki living is moderate by Nordic standards, leaving a fair surplus.

pros.text: Two tech salaries net a fair surplus, and Helsinki housing is more moderate than Oslo or Copenhagen.

cons.text: A 42.5% tax wedge (56.4% marginal for high earners) and ~€17k/yr tuition compress the study and early-career budget.

links.title: OECD — Taxing Wages 2025: Finland country note (PDF)

healthcare.summary: Residence-based universal healthcare (Kela) once settled, with high quality and outstanding family benefits, though students need insurance and there can be access gaps early on.

pros.text: Residence-based universal healthcare (Kela/municipal) once settled, with strong Nordic welfare and outstanding family benefits (childcare, the maternity package).

cons.text: Students must hold insurance and may face access gaps before full residence-based coverage applies; some treatment waits exist.

links.title: Study in Finland — life in Finland (healthcare, official)

culture-language.summary: Finns have excellent English and livability is world-leading, but Finnish/Swedish is required for status and a hard language to master, in a society with a tightening tone on immigration.

pros.text: Finns have excellent English (work and daily life are largely workable in English in Helsinki), and Finland tops global happiness/livability rankings with funded integration (kotoutuminen) support.

cons.text: Finnish/Swedish is required for PR and citizenship and is genuinely hard to learn, and the social tone toward immigrants has hardened under the Finns Party.

links.title: InfoFinland — settling in Finland (official)

safety-law.summary: Among the world's best for rule of law (WJP 2025: 3rd) and very safe, though Finland has documented racism issues affecting some minorities.

pros.text: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: 3rd worldwide; very low crime and strong women's safety, with reliable, low-corruption institutions.

cons.text: Finland has documented racism/discrimination issues affecting some visible minorities, and immigration enforcement has tightened.

links.title: World Justice Project — Rule of Law Index 2025

politics.summary: Very stable institutions, but the radical-right Finns Party sits in the governing coalition and is driving a restrictive turn across citizenship, PR and welfare access.

pros.text: Institutions are very stable, and skilled-talent attraction programmes remain in place.

cons.text: The radical-right Finns Party is in the governing coalition and is driving a restrictive paradigm shift (tighter citizenship, longer PR, welfare-linked permit revocation).
Finns Party rhetoric includes anti-immigration and anti-Islam strands, raising targeted risk for Muslim newcomers.

links.title: GLOBALCIT — Restrictive citizenship reform in Finland driven by the radical right

tax.summary: A high tax wedge (42.5%, 56.4% marginal) eased by a 32% flat foreign-expert regime for higher earners, but no confirmed Bangladesh treaty.

pros.text: A foreign-expert tax-at-source regime offers a flat 32% for up to 4 years to qualifying higher earners (salary above ~€5,800/month).

cons.text: The ordinary tax wedge is 42.5% (9th-highest OECD), 56.4% marginal for high earners, and no Finland–Bangladesh double-taxation treaty is confirmed.

links.title: OECD — Taxing Wages 2026: Finland country note (PDF)

muslim-diaspora.summary: A ~3% Muslim population (Somali/Arab/Turkish) with Helsinki prayer facilities and some halal, but no grand mosque (a proposal was rejected in 2017) and a small Bangladeshi community.

pros.text: Muslims are ~2.7–3% (~150,000–170,000); Helsinki has several mosques/Islamic centres (Masjid al-Iman, Helsinki Islamic Centre) and some halal food nearby.

cons.text: There is no grand mosque (a Helsinki proposal was rejected in 2017 amid politicised zoning and anti-Muslim opposition), the Bangladeshi community is small, and anti-Muslim sentiment is a documented concern.

links.title: HalalTrip — Guide to finding mosques in Finland
Islam / Somalis in Finland (overview)

direct-work-route.summary: One of the cleaner EU direct-hire routes for entry, with a settlement leg that tightened in 2024–26. The Specialist permit / EU Blue Card share a salary floor (€3,937/mo in 2026) easily cleared by Finnish dev pay, carry no labour-market test, and offer a 14-day fast-track plus a D-visa so an approved hire flies in immediately — both spouses can hold independent specialist permits. Finland actively recruits IT (Talent Boost; ~130,000 skilled-worker need this decade). The catch is onward: PR is now effectively 6 years (4 only with income/master's/language conditions, from Jan 2026) and citizenship 8 years (5 if Finnish/Swedish ~A2 is met). Dual citizenship is clean both ways. BD-side processing routes via the Embassy in New Delhi, lengthening the real timeline beyond the 2-week headline.

pros.text: Specialist permit / EU Blue Card with a 14-day fast-track + D-visa, no labour-market test; salary floor (€3,937/mo) easily cleared and both spouses can hold independent permits.
Finland actively recruits IT (Talent Boost; ~130,000 skilled-worker need this decade); dual citizenship retained both ways.

cons.text: Settlement leg tightened: PR effectively 6 years (4 only with income/master's/language gate, from Jan 2026); citizenship 8 years (5 only if Finnish/Swedish ~A2 met).
BD residence-permit biometrics route via the Embassy in New Delhi (not full processing in Dhaka), lengthening the real timeline; BD not a named Talent-Boost priority source.

Fast-track for EU Blue Card / specialists — Migri (official)
